How to Send a Pull Request on GitHub
Pull request is a fairly unique GitHub mechanism (GitLab has its own version too), but in this post I'll only walk through how to send a PR on GitHub.
GitHub hosts a ton of open-source projects. If you find one you'd like to contribute to, you can use the
(PR from here on) mechanism. The flow looks like this:
Fork it into your own repo and make changes
→ Push back to your own repo →
Send a PR to the original author
If you're not familiar with basic Git commands, check my earlier post:
Everything below uses my own alt account, so:
(Please don't actually send PRs to me — I won't review them.
Head over to the project repo and click Fork up top.
Once you click Fork you'll see this (it's copying...).
Step 2: Clone it locally and make changes
Grab the clone URL (make sure you're cloning from
fork, not the original — double-check the repo).
Once cloned, you'll see a new
Open it up and you'll find a README.md file. Edit it.
Step 4: Push back to your fork
Since we cloned from our fork, we can push straight back:

git add .
git commit -m 'I added a line to the README'
git push
git commit -m 'I added a line to the README'
And our online repo updates too.
Now for the actual PR. Head to your fork's repo page on GitHub and click PR.
, and confirm the target repo branch and your fork branch are both correct.
If everything looks good, click Create pull request.
The title will default to your last commit message. If you have anything specific to say to the author, leave a comment below.
After submitting you'll land on the original repo's PR page — the PR is sent.
If there's a problem with your branch, it'll tell you down here.
Now you wait for the author to review. If there are no issues, it'll get merged.
When someone sends a PR, you (the owner) get a notification (usually email). To handle it, head to the project and click PR.
Click in and you can click Commits up top to see what changed.
Click in to see the actual diff.
You can see they added a line.
Once you've reviewed and everything looks good, click Merge pull request.
Then decide whether to edit the merge message. When you're ready, click Confirm merge.
After clicking Confirm merge, the OPEN status flips to Merged — successful merge.
Head back to the project home and you'll see the contributor's PR is now part of the project.
What if there are multiple branches?

Most projects have at least two branches: Master (main branch) and Develop (development branch). Usually you don't PR directly to Master — you PR to Develop. After testing, the author merges Develop into Master. So pay attention to which branch you're PR'ing into.
That's pretty vague written out, so let's actually try it.
I'll skip the Fork & Clone screenshots since they're the same.
But for this case the clone command is slightly different:
This pulls the develop branch correctly.
After editing, the rest is the same — stage, commit, push.
Open your repo on GitHub — Master should be unchanged.
Switch to develop and you'll see your push.
The PR flow is basically the same as before, except for one thing — make sure you switch both your source and target branches.
Once that's set, send the PR. The rest of the flow is the same, except it'll go to the target project's develop branch.
This happens all the time in teams with more than one person — but resolving conflicts is a learning opportunity.
Let's say I forked the project.
In the meantime someone else edited the same file. When you try to PR, you'll see this:
You can still click Create pull request, but you'll get a heads-up that the README has a conflict.
Click Resolve conflicts and fix it in the GitHub web UI (warning: this takes forever — I waited almost half an hour before it loaded...)
Delete your forked repo and re-fork (this is what I recommend — fastest. Just copy the code out first.)
I've also got a post on resolving conflicts:
The bigger lesson though: conflicts are basically a communication problem. Try to coordinate so you don't edit the same file at the same time.
This whole flow is for PR'ing directly to open-source projects.
